common.companyBody:
In this project I worked as a PM at a B2B vehicle telemetry platform: hardware installed on vehicles plus a software dashboard that together give fleet managers visibility into location, driving behavior, maintenance and operations. The contract is recurring, sold to fleets of very different sizes, from small carriers to enterprise operations with thousands of vehicles.

After the sale, the customer goes through two stages before actually extracting value: physical installation of the devices, and dashboard configuration (drivers, alerts, geofences, groups). Only after that does the data turn into real fleet management. The company calls this initial stage activation.

basicValue.title: Basic Value: a metric to turn churn into activation — Vivian Notrispe
basicValue.heading: Basic Value: a metric to turn churn into activation
basicValue.team: Cross-functional squad — CS, Supply/Logistics and Product
basicValue.coverAlt: Line chart showing stable Basic Value growing from 35% to 51% of the base between October 2025 and June 2026.

context.body1: Reducing churn was one of the company's main strategic objectives for that cycle. To support that goal, I ran a discovery on early churn, looking into engagement data, usage patterns, and the customer experience in the first months of the contract.
context.body2: The analysis revealed different factors contributing to that scenario. Installation was one of the main points of friction, but another problem caught my attention: many customers who had already completed that step still struggled to get value from the product. On a platform with dozens of settings and features, it wasn't clear what the next steps were to start extracting value from the solution.
context.body3: Following that discovery, onboarding started being treated as a strategic workstream sponsored by a director and run in partnership with CS, Supply, and Scheduling. My challenge became understanding exactly where customers stopped progressing through the journey, and creating an objective way to measure onboarding quality.

discovery.body1: To understand why so many customers were abandoning the product in the first months, I combined data analysis, qualitative research, and direct observation of the implementation process.

methodDiagram.label: How I ran the discovery
methodDiagram.step2Item1: In-depth interviews with customers in onboarding
methodDiagram.caption: I cross-referenced quantitative churn and configuration data, qualitative research with customers, and direct observation of the installation and support process.

discovery.body2: Cross-referencing the last 12 months of churn data with each customer's configuration history, I found a consistent pattern: most customers who canceled their contract had never completed even 60% of the platform's basic configuration. It became clear the problem wasn't just retention, but customers' difficulty reaching a minimum level of activation before starting to get value.
discovery.bottlenecksIntro: The investigation revealed three bottlenecks that explained this behavior:
discovery.bottleneck1Title: Loss of context between Sales and Post-sales.
discovery.bottleneck1Body: Customers had to repeat information and restart part of the journey after signing the contract.
discovery.bottleneck2Body: Scheduling, vehicle unavailability, and no-shows delayed the start of operations.
discovery.bottleneck3Title: First access with high cognitive load.
discovery.bottleneck3Body: After installation, customers found dozens of settings and features with no clear indication of what was a priority.

process.body1: The main challenge was that the company had no objective way to answer a simple question: is this customer ready to get value yet? There were dozens of indicators scattered across Product, CS, and Supply, but none showed the real activation level of an account.
process.body2: To solve this, I defined and specified Basic Value, a metric calculated daily for each customer. It brought together four pillars of the journey — installation, setup, configuration, and continued use — into a score from 0 to 4, making it possible to track onboarding progress through a single indicator.
process.body3: Beyond prioritizing at-risk customers, Basic Value created a common language between Product, CS, and Supply. For the first time, every team tracked activation using the same criteria and goals.

compositionChart.label: Basic Value composition · from pillars to score
compositionChart.alt: Vertical diagram showing the four Basic Value pillars — installation, setup, configuration, and continued use — composing the final score from 0 to 4.
compositionChart.caption: Each pillar contributes a partial score to the final score, calculated daily per customer.

process.body4: After launching the metric, I ran a new round of qualitative research with the Customer Success team to understand why some customers reached a healthy Basic Value quickly while others stayed stuck.
process.body5: This research revealed problems the data alone couldn't explain. The biggest bottleneck was still installation. I also found that the internal checklist used by CS didn't hold up for larger operations, that some of the help content was outdated, and that the rescheduling process created friction right at the start of the customer relationship.
process.body6: These learnings helped prioritize the next initiatives and showed which Basic Value pillars needed to evolve first.

chart1.label: Basic Value pillars · average score (0–4)
chart1.alt: Bar chart showing the average score of the 4 Basic Value pillars: Installation 1.3, Setup 1.1, Basic configuration 0.7, Activation (heavy users) 0.3, on a 0 to 4 scale.
chart1.caption: Initial diagnosis across ~4,200 fleets analyzed. Installation and Setup were already pulling the average up; Configuration and, especially, usage engagement (Heavy Users) were the weakest links.

chart2.label: Feature adoption · % of fleets
chart2.alt: Bar chart showing feature adoption: registered drivers 87%, active users 75%, speed limit 60%, driver identification 54%, geofences 45%, configured groups 42%, fleet policies 38%, checklists 21%.
chart2.caption: Low adoption of checklists and fleet policies pointed to the two highest-priority spots for the next phase of the project.

solution.intro: With the diagnosis validated, the project moved forward on two fronts I helped specify:
solution.governancaBody: I aligned with Product, CS and Supply leadership on the official Basic Value criteria — what each pillar measures, how each team should track it by customer cohort, and a complementary "time to basic activation" metric — turning the metric into a common language between teams that previously each measured success their own way.
solution.onboardingBody: I wrote the PRD for an AI-guided progressive onboarding system that uses context collected at first access to automatically prioritize a single recommendation at a time — instead of overwhelming the customer with everything at once —, with advanced features locked until basic prerequisites are met, and increasingly personalized activation plans in later versions of the product.
solution.frentesIntro: Over the following semester, the initiatives actually executed were organized into three parallel fronts:
solution.produtoItem2: Registration flows guided by AI agents
solution.produtoItem3: Bulk registration to speed up setup for enterprise fleets
solution.csItem1: Extra campaigns and training with the customer base
solution.csItem2: A more structured onboarding process, with clear steps and owners
solution.supplyItem2: More scheduling visibility on the customer dashboard — what's left to install and how to schedule it becomes easier to see

result.body1: Basic Value stopped being a standalone product project and became a strategic goal shared by CS, Supply and Product, with its own working groups formed around it. In the first months after the first initiatives launched — activation checklist, completion indicators and an FAQ in the dashboard — engagement with activation tasks doubled year over year, and nearly half of the customers who interacted with these tasks for the first time completed at least one Basic Value step.
result.body2: When the goal was set, only 35% of the base had a stable Basic Value. We set a target of reaching 50% over the following semester — and, with a set of cross-functional initiatives across CS, Supply and Product, we hit 51%, beating the goal.

chart1.label: Stable Basic Value · % of MRR · Oct/25 – Jun/26
chart1.alt: Line chart showing the percentage of MRR with stable Basic Value rising from 35% in October 2025 to 51% in June 2026, beating the 50% goal.
chart1.caption: Month-by-month evolution of the percentage of MRR with stable Basic Value, from the initial diagnosis through the close of the initiatives semester.

chart2.label: Before → after · feature adoption
chart2.alt: Chart comparing feature adoption before and after the initiatives semester: registered drivers from 87% to 91%, active users from 75% to 88%, speed limit from 60% to 73%, driver identification from 54% to 46%, geofences from 45% to 67%, configured groups from 42% to 70%, fleet policies from 38% to 67%, checklists from 21% to 36%.
chart2.caption: Comparison by feature between the start of the diagnosis and the close of the initiatives semester. Almost every point advanced consistently — the exception was driver identification, which saw a slight drop, likely due to driver turnover within fleets.

result.closing: My biggest takeaway from this project was how a well-designed metric works as an organizational alignment tool, not just a product one: Basic Value gave historically disconnected teams — CS, logistics and product — a common language to prioritize what actually mattered for the customer to perceive value faster.

pedidos.title: Order Visibility: unblocking installation — Vivian Notrispe
pedidos.heading: Order Visibility: unblocking installation
pedidos.coverAlt: Illustration combining the order flow (order placed, shipping, scheduling and installation) with a simplified version of the orders page, showing the summary by order type and the status table.

context.body1: This project came out of a qualitative research finding from the Basic Value case: installation was the biggest activation bottleneck for customers, bigger even than the sales process or CS itself. I worked as PM and principal designer (PMX) on the Account Experience team, responsible for onboarding new customers.
context.body2: The platform setup depends on a physical step: installing telemetry devices on the fleet's vehicles or machinery, without which data doesn't start being read correctly. That means the customer has to wait for the device to arrive, schedule an installation date, count on a technician's availability on the requested date, and leave the vehicle out of operation, which affects fleet operations, until installation is complete. Only after that does the dashboard start populating with data and insights.

problem.body: Before this project, the customer had no operational visibility into their own order at all. To find out where the devices were, which hub would receive them, when they'd arrive so installation could be scheduled, or which order items had already been installed, the most common path was opening a ticket with support or CS. This wasn't just an annoyance: without this visibility, the fleet manager couldn't plan when to take vehicles out of operation for installation, couldn't organize operations on the agreed date, and couldn't track what was still pending for the next onboarding steps. Each of these questions generated a reactive contact, overloading the support team with information that already existed internally and had never been translated for the customer.

process.body1: I brought this pain point to the team as a formal initiative, together with a design intern who was in her early days on the team. We started by mapping, on a collaborative board, the end-to-end onboarding and installation flow and every point where the customer lost visibility, from ordering the device to completed installation.
process.diagIntro: The diagnosis pointed to four specific causes for the lack of clarity:
process.diagItem1: The scheduled installation date was hidden inside the flow, while the devices' arrival date, less relevant to the customer at that point, was more prominent on screen.
process.diagItem2: Each step of the process (shipping, installation, return) lived in a separate internal ticket, with no visible connection between them.
process.diagItem3: The customer dashboard had no process with a beginning, middle and end, and each step appeared broken up across different tabs.
process.diagItem4: Underlying all of this, the customer remained dependent on the CSM to translate these internal tickets into information that made sense to them.

chart1.label: Order flow · where the customer depended on CS
chart1.alt: Diagram of the order flow in four steps (order placed, device shipping, scheduling and installation), showing that in each of the last three steps the customer had to contact CS due to lack of visibility.
chart1.caption: Mapping the onboarding-installation flow: in three of the four order steps, the lack of visibility was only resolved through a reactive contact to CS.

process.body2: Working with Hubspot and operational processes outside the product sphere was a challenge of its own. We found gaps that couldn't be solved on the front end alone, because they came from larger operations the team didn't control, and shipping, installation and return data lived in different pipelines, with no single point connecting them. To deal with this, we specified a new API that would consolidate this information before translating it for the customer, and we trimmed down a good part of these internal steps without compromising the visibility the customer saw on their side.

solution.body1: The central deliverable was a new orders page, bringing together in one place device purchases, exchanges and returns, today scattered across separate internal tickets, along with a quick summary of how many orders are in each state and a scheduling recommendation whenever there's a pending installation.
solution.img1Alt: Orders page screen showing summary cards for orders in progress, purchases, exchanges and returns, and the orders table with stage, scheduled date and deadline.
solution.img1Caption: Overview of the orders page, with a summary by order type and an automatic recommendation to schedule pending installations.
solution.body2: From this overview, each order opens into a detailed status timeline, translating internal operations statuses into simple wording the customer can follow on their own: order placed, device shipping, installation. Each step shows address, arrival estimate and proposed scheduling dates, with no need for the customer to open a ticket to find this out.
solution.img2Alt: Orders page screen showing the order summary and installation timeline, with each attempt detailing the related ticket, address and arrival estimate.
solution.img2Caption: The timeline translates internal operations statuses (Hubspot tickets) into wording the customer understands on their own, without contacting support.
solution.body3: Alongside the timeline, we designed a per-device detail view, which solves the most common CS question: orders with multiple devices and different installation dates, where the customer just wanted to know which ones had already been installed.
solution.img3Alt: Orders page screen showing a table with the individual status of each device in the order, associated vehicle, delivery address and related ticket.
solution.img3Caption: Detailed per-device view, built for orders with multiple items and different installation dates.
solution.body4: As part of the same effort, the team also delivered a scheduling shortcut directly from the orders page, connected to an AI scheduling agent, a delivery I'll cover in a separate case.

result.stat1Label: of fleets accessing the orders page in June (started from 0% before launch)
result.stat2Label: of schedulings done directly through the dashboard, without support
result.body1: Before launch, we set a goal of 20% adoption of the new orders page within the first three months. We launched to part of the base in February and rolled out to 100% of customers in March. From there, adoption jumped from 0% to a peak of 35% in May, settling at 32% in June, comfortably beating the goal. Alongside this, 40% of schedulings started being done directly through the dashboard, without going through support. This freed up the CS team from a meaningful volume of reactive contacts that existed only because the information already existed internally and had never been translated for the customer.

chart1.label: Orders page access · % of fleets · Jan-Jun/26
chart1.alt: Line chart showing orders page access rising from 0% in January and February to 21% in March, 29% in April, a peak of 35% in May, and settling at 32% in June, beating the 20% goal.
chart1.caption: We launched to part of the base in February and rolled out to 100% of customers in March.

result.closing: The clearest takeaway from this project was that a good part of the support load doesn't come from real product problems, but from information that already exists internally and was never translated for the user. Making that information visible to the customer is what actually reduces friction and dependency on support.

recomendacoes.title: Smart Recommendations: the deterministic ruleset behind the AI — Vivian Notrispe
recomendacoes.heading: Smart Recommendations: the deterministic ruleset behind the AI
recomendacoes.role: PM + Principal UX (PMX) — DRI for the prioritization logic and trigger engine
recomendacoes.team: Account Experience squad — PO, engineers and a data/AI scientist
recomendacoes.duration: ~5 months (discovery → POC → customer testing → gradual rollout)
recomendacoes.tools: Mastra AI, LLM for personalization, Mixpanel, Figma
recomendacoes.coverAlt: Diagram of the recommendation engine: dashboard and CRM signals feed a deterministic engine with health status and a criticality ruleset, which decides the recommendation level before an LLM personalizes the final message. Below, an example shows installation_pct 65% and 9 days without movement classified as yellow status and urgent tier, generating the message 'you already have 65% of your fleet installed, let's schedule the next batch'.

context.body1: This project came about as an extension of the onboarding studies. During that discovery, I developed a metric adapted from Basic Value, used to measure a company's basic activation percentage, i.e. how much of the product it has already set up to start generating value. Today, that metric is used as one of the main gauges of early churn risk.
context.body2: Beyond the quantitative data, qualitative research revealed another important point: even after activating the platform, many customers didn't know what the next steps were. There was a lack of clarity about the tool's potential, understanding of the available features, and practical guidance to help evolve usage.
context.body3: The problem wasn't lack of interest. The platform offers dozens of configuration possibilities and, with no prioritization at all, everything seemed equally important. The result was a high cognitive load right from the first sessions, making activation harder and reducing product engagement.
context.body4: I worked as PM and principal UX (PMX) on the Account Experience team, and this was the second workstream I took on as DRI after Basic Value: a recommendation engine that, based on what had already been done in the dashboard and what was still missing for Basic Value, decides at every moment which next action makes sense to show that specific customer.

problem.body1: Leadership's direction was to apply AI to the solutions, with no defined scope of where or how. It quickly became clear that letting an LLM decide on its own what to recommend and when was a risky bet: we have no way to audit why the model recommended X to customer Y, nor to guarantee it never shows two competing recommendations in the same session. That kind of overload was exactly what the Basic Value research had already flagged as a cause of abandonment.
problem.body2: The real product problem, then, wasn't generating personalized text: it was deciding, in a deterministic and auditable way, which recommendation wins in each context, before any LLM enters the picture. That was the part of the project I took on as my main focus.

process.intro: I mapped each of the journeys that make up Basic Value (Installation and Setup + Configuration, in this first version) into objective signals already available in the dashboard and CRM: percentage of vehicles installed, days without any relevant movement, whether the next installation batch already had a scheduled date, how many of the 9 Setup criteria had already been met, among others. On top of these signals, I specified two layers of rules:
process.item1Body: Classifies each account as Red, Yellow, Blue or Green, always evaluated in that order: the first true condition wins. Example: devices delivered 10 or more days ago with no scheduling falls straight into Red, regardless of any other signal.
process.item2Body: Translates each specific operational situation into a recommendation level (Critical, Urgent, Important or Suggestive), each with its own UI component and cooldown rule.
process.item3Body: At most 1 Critical, Urgent or Important recommendation active at a time: if Installation is at Critical, Setup is automatically downgraded to Suggestive, even if it would deserve a higher level on its own.
process.item4Body: Every resolved condition triggers immediate suppression of the recommendation, without waiting for the next cycle. Each level also has its own progressive escalation after repeated dismissals, to avoid fatigue.

chart1.label: Criticality tier · hierarchy and cooldown
chart1.alt: Diagram of the criticality tier with four levels: Critical, no cooldown, reappears on every login, example devices delivered 10+ days ago with no scheduling; Urgent, 24-hour cooldown, example progress stalled 7 to 14 days with no scheduling; Important, 48-hour cooldown, example new installation batch completed; Suggestive, 7-day cooldown, example fleet approaching the installation threshold.
chart1.critDesc: Devices delivered 10+ days ago with no scheduling
chart1.urgDesc: Progress stalled 7–14 days with no scheduling
chart1.caption: Each level has its own UI component (modal, banner, floating card, contextual) and its own cooldown rule. The more critical, the less tolerance for being ignored.

process.body2: Only after this rules layer decides what and when to recommend does an LLM step in to personalize the message (customer name, fleet size, specific counts), never the decision itself.
process.body3: Before exposing this to real customers, we validated the logic in isolation: we ran the engine over data from a real fleet, using Mastra AI to orchestrate the LLM call, and compared the output against what the deterministic ruleset predicted. This step also answered operational questions that only surface once the logic actually exists: how many LLM calls per customer this generates, and whether it makes sense to run the engine on every dashboard visit or only periodically (we chose periodic execution, not real-time, for cost reasons). Next, we ran E2E tests with real customers in A/B groups via feature flag, which surfaced concrete problems before the wide rollout: camera recommendations showing up for fleets without an installed camera, or a floating card duplicating a suggestion that had already been dismissed in another component on the same screen.

solution.body1: In practice, this logic translates into four UI components with decreasing intrusiveness: a blocking modal for Critical, a persistent banner for Urgent, a floating card for Important, and a contextual recommendation within the flow for Suggestive. The home screen also changes depending on the fleet's stage. I won't go deep into this experience layer here because it wasn't my main focus in this project; the ruleset and prioritization above are what support any of these screens.
solution.img1Alt: Dashboard home showing fleet installation progress and a high-priority floating recommendation card suggesting a review of the AI-generated driver behavior ranking.
solution.img1Caption: The floating card appears over the home screen, prioritized by the criticality tier, without blocking the customer's navigation.
solution.img2Alt: Vehicles page showing a contextual recommendation suggesting creating points of interest from recurring stops detected by AI.
solution.img2Caption: Contextual recommendations appear within the usage flow itself: here, suggesting the creation of points of interest from the fleet's recurring stops.
solution.body2: We chose to launch gradually instead of releasing to the whole base at once: in March, the engine ran only for the slice of fleets with the lowest Basic Value (about 10% of the base), and we opened it up to the rest over April and May, monitoring behavior before exposing the entire base.

result.stat1Label: clicks on recommendation CTAs between March and June (started from zero before rollout)
result.stat2Label: of Enterprise fleets with complete basic configuration (52% → 86% of MRR)
result.body1: The gradual rollout took about two months to gain traction. Starting in April, when most of the base already had access to the engine, clicks on recommendation CTAs jumped from near zero to over 7,000 events by June, with about 1,200 unique fleets interacting at least once.

chart1.label: AI recommendation engagement · CTA clicks · Jan-Jul/26
chart1.alt: Line chart showing total clicks on recommendation CTAs and unique fleets that clicked, from January to July 2026. Total clicks go from zero in February, rising to about 2,000 in April, 4,000 in May and 7,100 in June, with a dotted projection of 7,500 in July. Unique fleets follow the same curve at a smaller scale, from zero to 1,200 in June and 1,300 projected for July.
chart1.caption: *July partial at time of collection. Rollout started with ~10% of the base in March, expanded through April and May.

result.body2: The recommendation that converted the most, consistently, was basic configuration (Setup + Config): drivers, alerts, POIs, groups, speed limit. Between October 2025 and June 2026, the percentage of MRR with these configurations completed rose across every fleet size, but the most visible jump came after April, once the engine started covering most of the base: Enterprise accounts went from 52% to 86%, Medium from 28% to 52%, and SMB from 11% to 24%.

chart2.label: Basic configuration completed · % of MRR by fleet size · Oct/25-Jun/26
chart2.alt: Line chart showing the percentage of MRR with basic configuration completed by fleet size. Enterprise rises from 52% in October 2025 to 86% in June 2026. Medium rises from 28% to 52%. SMB rises from 11% to 24%. All three curves accelerate starting in April 2026.
chart2.caption: All three curves follow similar trajectories, but the slope changes visibly starting in April, when the engine started covering most of the base.

result.body3: The effect on the previous case's north-star metric was also visible: stable Basic Value, which had been oscillating between 36% and 40% for six months without moving, broke through that plateau in the exact window when the engine started covering most of the base, closing June at 50%.

chart3.label: Stable Basic Value · % of MRR · Oct/25-Jun/26
chart3.alt: Line chart showing stable Basic Value oscillating between 36% and 40% from October 2025 to March 2026, then rising to 50% in June 2026, coinciding with the recommendation engine rollout expansion starting in April.
chart3.caption: The metric was stuck for six months and broke through the plateau in the same window the recommendation engine started covering most of the base. It's a coincidence in timing, not proof of isolated causality, but a strong signal given the alignment with the rollout.

result.closing: My biggest takeaway from this project was that, in a recommendation engine, the deterministic part is what sustains the team's trust, not the generative part. An auditable ruleset makes it possible to explain why a recommendation appeared, predict its behavior before simulating with the LLM, and build guardrails before any text generation; AI personalization only has room to work well once a layer like this already exists deciding what and when to recommend.
